response_text:
# The Deep End of Millford

Every summer in Millford, Ohio, the public pool on Elm Street became our town's beating heart—a cracked concrete basin where kids cannonballed into the water and parents chain-smoked on the splintered bleachers. I was eight the first time my dad dropped me off there, his pickup truck kicking up gravel as he yelled, "Don't drown, kid!" through the open window. Clutching a faded beach towel with cartoon sharks on it, I pushed through the chain-link gate, the lifeguard's whistle piercing the humid air like a starter pistol. That pool wasn't just a place to cool off; it was Millford's unfiltered mirror, reflecting the town's stubborn pride, its quiet fractures, and the way we all pretended not to notice the stains.

Millford was the kind of Rust Belt town that time forgot after the steel mill shuttered in '82. Population: 4,200 souls, give or take a factory layoff. We had a single stoplight downtown, a VFW hall that doubled as prom venue, and enough Baptist churches to host a tent revival on every corner. On the surface, it was all Norman Rockwell: Friday night fish fries, high school football under floodlights, neighbors waving from porch swings. But the pool stripped away the postcards. It was open to all—whites, Blacks, a smattering of Latino families who'd trickled in from the fields south of town—and for two months a year, it forced us into the same shallow end.

The shallow end was kid territory, a wading pool of splashes and shouts where hierarchies dissolved faster than sunscreen. I'd spend hours there with Tommy Ruiz, whose dad picked tomatoes at the co-op, building forts out of pool noodles while the older boys dared each other off the diving board. Tommy taught me the Mexican folk song his mom sang while scrubbing laundry, and I showed him how to flick a bottle cap like a hockey puck. We didn't think about skin color or whose family got the pink slip last; we just raced to the snack bar for fifteen-cent Popsicles that dripped red down our chins. It was pure, that innocence—the pool's magic trick of making strangers into allies under the relentless Ohio sun.

But venture to the deep end, and Millford's undercurrents bubbled up. That's where the teenagers held court, their transistor radios blasting Springsteen or Madonna, flirting and flexing like it was their personal prom. I remember
